672	\subsection{Substrate lemmas for Stacks 00OE and the scheme bridges}
673	\label{subsec:codim1_substrate_lemmas}
674	
675	The narrative above is realised on the Lean side by the named substrate lemmas
676	collected here. They feed the Stacks-00OE Krull-dimension formula
677	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} and the regular-stalk assembly.
678	
679	\paragraph{Stacks 00OE localisation/height bridge.}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[00OE Step 1 --- localised Krull dimension equals height]
683	  \label{lem:ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime}
684	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime}
685	  For a prime ideal \(\mathfrak m\) of a commutative ring \(R\) and any localisation
686	  \(A\) of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak m\), the Krull dimension of \(A\) equals the height of
687	  \(\mathfrak m\).
688	\end{lemma}
689	
690	\begin{proof}
691	  Proved directly in Lean: re-export of Mathlib's
692	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}. Consumed by the
693	  closed-point dimension bound
694	  \cref{lem:standard_smooth_le_ringKrullDim_localization}.
695	\end{proof}
696	
697	\paragraph{Closed-point form of the 00OE formula (Krull height theorem route).}
698	
699	The following route proves the localised Krull-dimension formula at a maximal
700	ideal unconditionally: the dimension \emph{lower} bound comes from the full
701	height of the pulled-back maximal ideal in the polynomial ring via Krull's
702	height theorem, and the \emph{upper} bound is automatic from the cotangent
703	space. Combined with the
704	cotangent computation \cref{lem:finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_residue},
705	this proves regularity of a standard-smooth algebra at every closed point.
706	
707	\begin{lemma}\leanok
708	[Maximal ideals of polynomial rings over a field have full height]
709	  \label{lem:mvPolynomial_height_eq_natCard_of_isMaximal}
710	  \lean{MvPolynomial.height_eq_natCard_of_isMaximal}
711	  For a field \(k\) and a finite index set \(I\), every maximal ideal \(M\) of
712	  the polynomial ring \(k[X_i : i \in I]\) has height exactly \(\#I\).
713	\end{lemma}
714	
715	\begin{proof}
716	  Induction on \(\#I\). For \(I = \emptyset\) the ring is the field \(k\), of
717	  Krull dimension \(0\), so every prime has height \(0\). For the induction
718	  step write \(k[X_i : i \in I \sqcup \{\ast\}] = R[X]\) with
719	  \(R = k[X_i : i \in I]\). Given a maximal ideal \(M \subseteq R[X]\), its
720	  contraction \(\mathfrak p = M \cap R\) is again maximal, because \(R\) is a
721	  finitely generated algebra over a field and hence a Jacobson ring. For a
722	  maximal ideal of \(R[X]\) over a Noetherian ring \(R\) one has
723	  \(\operatorname{ht} M = \operatorname{ht} \mathfrak p + 1\): the fibre
724	  \(R[X] \otimes_R \kappa(\mathfrak p) = \kappa(\mathfrak p)[X]\) is a
725	  principal ideal domain, so the image of \(M\) there has height \(1\), and
726	  going-down along the flat extension \(R \to R[X]\) splices the chains. The
727	  claim follows from the inductive hypothesis
728	  \(\operatorname{ht}\mathfrak p = \#I\).
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\begin{lemma}\leanok
732	[Height lower bound at maximal ideals of standard-smooth algebras]
733	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_le_height_of_isMaximal}
734	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.natCast_le_height_of_isMaximal}
735	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(S\) a standard-smooth \(k\)-algebra of relative
736	  dimension \(n\). Then every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m \subseteq S\) has
737	  height at least \(n\).
738	\end{lemma}
739	
740	\begin{proof}
741	  \uses{lem:mvPolynomial_height_eq_natCard_of_isMaximal}
742	  Choose a submersive presentation \(S = P/I\) with
743	  \(P = k[X_1, \dots, X_N]\) and \(I = (f_1, \dots, f_c)\) generated by the
744	  \(c\) relations, so that \(n = N - c\); the submersive structure embeds the
745	  relation indices into the generator indices, so \(c \le N\) and
746	  \(n + c = N\). The preimage \(M \subseteq P\) of \(\mathfrak m\) is a maximal
747	  ideal containing \(I\), of height \(N\) by
748	  \cref{lem:mvPolynomial_height_eq_natCard_of_isMaximal}. Krull's height
749	  theorem, in the relative form
750	  \(\operatorname{ht} M \le \operatorname{ht}(M/I) + \mu(I)\) where
751	  \(\mu(I) \le c\) is the minimal number of generators of \(I\), gives
752	  \(N \le \operatorname{ht}(\mathfrak m) + c\) since \(M/I\) corresponds to
753	  \(\mathfrak m\) under \(P/I \simeq S\). Hence
754	  \(n \le \operatorname{ht}\mathfrak m\).
755	\end{proof}
756	
757	\begin{lemma}\leanok
758	[Dimension lower bound at closed points of standard-smooth algebras]
759	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_le_ringKrullDim_localization}
760	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.le_ringKrullDim_of_isLocalization_atPrime}
761	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(S\) a standard-smooth \(k\)-algebra of relative
762	  dimension \(n\), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq S\) a maximal ideal, and
763	  \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) a localisation of \(S\) at \(\mathfrak m\). Then
764	  \(n \le \dim S_{\mathfrak m}\).
765	\end{lemma}
766	
767	\begin{proof}
768	  \uses{lem:standard_smooth_le_height_of_isMaximal,
769	        lem:ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime}
770	  Combine \(\dim S_{\mathfrak m} = \operatorname{ht}\mathfrak m\)
771	  (\cref{lem:ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime}) with the height
772	  lower bound \cref{lem:standard_smooth_le_height_of_isMaximal}.
773	\end{proof}
774	
775	\begin{lemma}\leanok
776	[Regularity from the cotangent upper bound]
777	  \label{lem:regular_of_finrank_cotangent_le_dim}
778	  \lean{IsRegularLocalRing.of_finrank_cotangentSpace_le_ringKrullDim}
779	  Let \(A\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and
780	  residue field \(\kappa\). If
781	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \le \dim A\), then \(A\) is a
782	  regular local ring.
783	\end{lemma}
784	
785	\begin{proof}
786	  By Nakayama's lemma the minimal number of generators \(\mu(\mathfrak m)\)
787	  equals \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\), and Krull's height theorem
788	  gives \(\dim A \le \mu(\mathfrak m)\) in any Noetherian local ring. The
789	  hypothesis forces \(\mu(\mathfrak m) = \dim A\), which is the definition of
790	  regularity.
791	\end{proof}
792	
793	\begin{lemma}\leanok
794	[Stacks 00TT at a rational closed point, algebra level]
795	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_rational_point}
796	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_localization_of_isStandardSmooth_of_bijective_residue}
797	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(S\) a standard-smooth \(k\)-algebra,
798	  \(\mathfrak m \subseteq S\) a maximal ideal, and \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) a
799	  localisation of \(S\) at \(\mathfrak m\) such that the composite
800	  \(k \to S_{\mathfrak m} \to \kappa(\mathfrak m)\) is bijective (a
801	  \(k\)-rational point). Then \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) is a regular local ring.
802	\end{lemma}
803	
804	\begin{proof}
805	  \uses{lem:standard_smooth_le_ringKrullDim_localization,
806	        lem:regular_of_finrank_cotangent_le_dim,
807	        lem:finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_residue,
808	        lem:module_free_kaehler_standardSmooth,
809	        lem:module_free_kaehler_localization,
810	        lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
811	        lem:exists_standardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_of_standardSmooth}
812	  Let \(n\) be the relative dimension of \(S\) over \(k\)
813	  (\cref{lem:exists_standardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_of_standardSmooth}).
814	  The ring \(S\) is Noetherian (finite type over a field), hence so is
815	  \(S_{\mathfrak m}\), and \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) is formally smooth over \(k\)
816	  (a localisation of a smooth algebra). Its K\"ahler module
817	  \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak m}/k}\) is free of rank \(n\)
818	  (\cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_standardSmooth},
819	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy, lem:av_indeterminacy_empty}
54	
55	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
56	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
57	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
58	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
59	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
60	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
61	
62	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
63	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
64	  map of \(\bar k\)-varieties (defined over \(\bar k\)).
65	  Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
66	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
67	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
68	\end{theorem}
69	
70	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
71	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
72	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
73	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
74	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
75	
76	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
77	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
78	\begin{proof}
79	  
80	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
81	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
82	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
83	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
84	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
85	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
86	
87	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
88	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
89	  \begin{enumerate}
90	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
91	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
92	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
93	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
94	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
95	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
96	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
97	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
98	  \end{enumerate}
99	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
100	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
101	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
102	\end{proof}
103	
104	\begin{remark}
105	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
106	  \textbf{No depth input is needed.} The combination above runs entirely through
107	  the valuative criterion of properness (inside
108	  \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}, applied at the DVR \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of
109	  each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal and \(A\) is
110	  complete) and the formal extension theorem \cref{thm:codim_one_extension},
111	  which needs only that the indeterminacy locus is \emph{empty}. In particular
112	  the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and its
113	  Cohen--Macaulay corollary \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
114	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} are \emph{not} consumed by this chain:
115	  a depth-\(\geq 2\) local-cohomology argument would be required only for
116	  Weil-style extension theorems that extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points
117	  \emph{without} first emptying the locus, and codim-\(1\)-freeness alone cannot
118	  yield an extension for a general complete target
119	  (\cref{rmk:codim1_no_extension_from_codim1free}).
120	\end{remark}
121	
122	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
123	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
124	
125	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
126	\begin{itemize}
127	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
128	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
129	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
130	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
131	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
132	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
133	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
134	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
135	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
136	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
137	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
138	    has a characteristic restriction.
139	\end{itemize}
140	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
141	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
142	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
143	
144	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
145	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
146	
147	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
148	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
149	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
150	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
151	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
152	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
153	defined by
154	\[
155	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
156	\]
157	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
158	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
159	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
160	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
161	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
162	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
163	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
164	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
165	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \texttt{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
166	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
167	
168	\section{Lean encoding}
169	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
170	
171	The Lean target file is the new
172	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
173	this chapter targets is the theorem
174	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
175	notation):
176	\[
177	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
178	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
179	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
180	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
181	\]
182	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
183	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
184	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
185	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
186	in the three-step pattern above (codim \(\geq 2\) from A.4.a + pure-codim \(1\) from A.4.a
187	\(\Rightarrow\) empty indeterminacy locus).
188	
189	The Mathlib namespace target is
190	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
191	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
192	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
193	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
194	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
195	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
196	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
197	
198	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
199	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
200	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
201	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
202	
203	\section{Internal Lean helper lemmas}
204	\label{sec:thm32_lean_helpers}
205	
206	The Lean proof of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} factors through a small
207	chain of internal helper lemmas that decompose the two-input combination above
208	into self-contained, separately-checkable pieces. These carry no external
209	mathematical source; each is a packaging step internal to the formalisation.
210	
211	\begin{lemma}\leanok
212	[A scheme smooth over an algebraically closed field is reduced]
213	  \label{lem:isReduced_of_smooth_over_field}
214	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.isReduced_of_smooth_over_field}
215	  For an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and an object \(A\) over
216	  \(\Spec \bar k\) whose structure morphism \(A.\mathrm{hom}\) is smooth, the
217	  underlying scheme \(A.\mathrm{left}\) is reduced.
218	  \begin{proof}
219	    \uses{lem:smooth_scheme_reduced_algclosed}
220	    Immediate from \cref{lem:smooth_scheme_reduced_algclosed}.
221	  \end{proof}
222	\end{lemma}
223	
224	\begin{lemma}\leanok
225	[Integrality of the abelian variety]
226	  \label{lem:av_isIntegral_of_smooth_geomIrred}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.av_isIntegral_of_smooth_geomIrred}
228	  \uses{lem:isReduced_of_smooth_over_field}
229	  For an abelian variety \(A\) over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\)
230	  (encoded as \(A\) over \(\Spec \bar k\) with group-object, proper, smooth, and
231	  geometrically irreducible structure), the underlying scheme \(A.\mathrm{left}\)
232	  is integral: it is irreducible (geometric irreducibility over the singleton
233	  base \(\Spec \bar k\)) and reduced
234	  (\cref{lem:isReduced_of_smooth_over_field}).
235	  \begin{proof}
236	    Proved directly in Lean.
237	  \end{proof}
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
241	[The indeterminacy locus of a rational map to an abelian variety is empty]
242	  \label{lem:av_indeterminacy_empty}
243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.av_indeterminacyLocus_eq_empty}
244	  \uses{lem:av_isIntegral_of_smooth_geomIrred, lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy, thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}
245	  For a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\), defined over \(\bar k\),
246	  from a nonsingular variety \(X\) to an abelian variety \(A\) over an
247	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), the indeterminacy locus of \(f\) is
248	  empty. Indeed, by the pure-codim-\(1\)-or-empty dichotomy of Milne's
249	  Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}, applied with
250	  \(A.\mathrm{left}\) integral by \cref{lem:av_isIntegral_of_smooth_geomIrred},
251	  since an abelian variety is in particular a group variety), the locus is
252	  empty or contains a point of codimension \(1\); the latter contradicts the
253	  codim-\(\geq 2\) conclusion of Milne's Theorem~3.1
254	  (\cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}).
255	  \begin{proof}
256	    Proved directly in Lean.
257	  \end{proof}
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{remark}
261	  \label{rmk:thm32_why_empty_not_codim1free}
262	  The chain deliberately runs through ``the indeterminacy locus is
263	  \emph{empty}'' rather than through codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-freeness: for a
264	  merely complete target, codim-\(1\)-freeness does \emph{not} imply the
265	  existence of a regular extension (see
266	  \cref{rmk:codim1_no_extension_from_codim1free}). Emptiness of the locus is
267	  exactly the hypothesis of the extension theorem
268	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}.
269	\end{remark}
270	
271	\section{Out of scope}
272	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
273	
274	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
275	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
276	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
277	\begin{itemize}
278	  \item \textbf{The codim-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself,
279	    and the extension theorem from an empty indeterminacy locus.} Proved in
280	    \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}
281	    and \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes them.
282	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
283	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
284	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
285	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
286	    (Milne~\(\equiv\) Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
287	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
288	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; the present chain no longer consumes them
289	    (no depth or local-cohomology input is needed once the indeterminacy locus
290	    is shown empty; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
291	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
292	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
293	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
294	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
295	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) is constant'',
296	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
297	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
298	    current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case
299	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
300	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
301	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
302	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
303	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
304	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
305	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
306	    \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to \(C^{(g)} \to A\).
307	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
308	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
309	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout (\texttt{chap:RigidityKbar}).
310	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
311	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
312	\end{itemize}
313

828	
829	\begin{lemma}\leanok
830	[Stacks 00TT at closed points over an algebraically closed field]
831	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_closed_point_algclosed}
832	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_localizationAtPrime_of_isStandardSmooth_of_isAlgClosed}
833	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(S\) a standard-smooth
834	  \(\bar k\)-algebra. Then for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m \subseteq S\)
835	  the local ring \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) is regular.
836	\end{lemma}
837	
838	\begin{proof}
839	  \uses{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_rational_point}
840	  By Zariski's lemma the residue field \(S/\mathfrak m\) is a finite, hence
841	  algebraic, extension of \(\bar k\); as \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed,
842	  \(\bar k \to S/\mathfrak m\) is bijective, and composing with the
843	  isomorphism \(S/\mathfrak m \simeq \kappa(\mathfrak m)\) (valid since
844	  \(\mathfrak m\) is maximal) shows every maximal ideal is a
845	  \(\bar k\)-rational point. Conclude with
846	  \cref{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_rational_point}.
847	\end{proof}
848	
849	\begin{lemma}\leanok
850	[Step B.e --- standard-smooth algebras over an algebraically closed field are reduced]
851	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_reduced_algclosed}
852	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isReduced_of_isStandardSmooth_of_isAlgClosed}
853	  \uses{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_closed_point_algclosed,
854	        lem:isDomain_of_regularLocal}
855	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(S\) a standard-smooth
856	  \(\bar k\)-algebra. Then \(S\) is reduced.
857	\end{lemma}
858	
859	\begin{proof}
860	  If \(S\) is the zero ring the claim is vacuous, so assume \(S \neq 0\).
861	  Reducedness is detected at localisations at maximal ideals: if \(x \in S\) is
862	  nilpotent and nonzero, its annihilator is a proper ideal, hence contained in
863	  some maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\); the image of \(x\) in \(S_{\mathfrak m}\)
864	  is then nilpotent and nonzero, so \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) is not reduced. It
865	  therefore suffices to show every \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) is reduced. By
866	  \cref{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_closed_point_algclosed} the local ring
867	  \(S_{\mathfrak m}\) is regular, by \cref{lem:isDomain_of_regularLocal} it is
868	  an integral domain, and integral domains are reduced.
869	\end{proof}
870	
871	\begin{lemma}\leanok
872	[Step B.f --- a scheme smooth over an algebraically closed field is reduced]
873	  \label{lem:smooth_scheme_reduced_algclosed}
874	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isReduced_of_smooth_of_isAlgClosed}
875	  \uses{lem:exists_standardSmooth_at_of_smooth, lem:gammaSpecField_ringEquiv,
876	        lem:isLocalization_atPrime_stalk_of_affineOpen,
877	        lem:standard_smooth_reduced_algclosed}
878	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field and let \(X\) be a scheme with
879	  a smooth morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\). Then \(X\) is reduced.
880	\end{lemma}
881	
882	\begin{proof}
883	  A scheme is reduced if and only if all of its stalks are reduced, so fix
884	  \(z \in X\). By \cref{lem:exists_standardSmooth_at_of_smooth} there are affine
885	  opens \(U \subseteq \Spec(\bar k)\) and \(V \ni z\) with \(V\) mapping into
886	  \(U\) such that \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\) is standard
887	  smooth. Since \(U\) contains the image of \(z\) it is nonempty, so
888	  \cref{lem:gammaSpecField_ringEquiv} identifies
889	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k, U) \simeq \bar k\); transporting the presentation along
890	  this isomorphism makes \(\Gamma(X, V)\) a standard-smooth
891	  \(\bar k\)-algebra, which is reduced by
892	  \cref{lem:standard_smooth_reduced_algclosed}. The stalk
893	  \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\) is a localisation of \(\Gamma(X, V)\) by
894	  \cref{lem:isLocalization_atPrime_stalk_of_affineOpen}, and a localisation of
895	  a reduced ring is reduced.
896	\end{proof}
897	
898	\paragraph{Relative dimension and scheme-to-algebra bridges.}
899	
900	\begin{lemma}\leanok
901	[Stage 6 sub-gap (i) --- relative dimension of a standard-smooth algebra]
902	  \label{lem:exists_standardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_of_standardSmooth}
903	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_of_isStandardSmooth}
904	  Every standard-smooth \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is standard smooth of some specific
905	  relative dimension \(n \in \mathbb N\).
906	\end{lemma}
907	
908	\begin{proof}
909	  Proved directly in Lean: read the dimension off the underlying submersive
910	  presentation. A sub-step of \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}.
911	\end{proof}
912	
913	\begin{lemma}\leanok
914	[Step B.a --- submersive presentation of a relative-dimension-$n$ algebra]
915	  \label{lem:exists_submersivePresentation_of_standardSmoothReldim}
916	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_submersivePresentation_of_isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}
917	  \uses{lem:exists_standardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_of_standardSmooth}
918	  An \(R\)-algebra \(S\) that is standard smooth of relative dimension \(n\) admits an
919	  explicit submersive presentation \(P\) with \(P.\mathrm{dimension} = n\).
920	\end{lemma}
921	
922	\begin{proof}
923	  Proved directly in Lean: re-export of the underlying-presentation field of the
924	  relative-dimension instance.
925	\end{proof}
926	
927	\begin{lemma}\leanok
928	[Step B.b --- affine-open stalk is a localisation]
929	  \label{lem:isLocalization_atPrime_stalk_of_affineOpen}
930	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isLocalization_atPrime_stalk_of_affineOpen}
931	  For an affine open \(V \ni z\) of a scheme \(X\), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\) is
932	  the localisation of the section ring \(\Gamma(X, V)\) at the prime ideal of \(z\).
933	\end{lemma}
934	
935	\begin{proof}
936	  Proved directly in Lean: re-export of \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}.
937	\end{proof}
938	
939	\begin{lemma}\leanok
940	[Step B.c helper --- nonempty open on a one-point space is everything]
941	  \label{lem:open_eq_top_of_subsingleton}
942	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.open_eq_top_of_subsingleton}
943	  On a scheme whose underlying space is a subsingleton (at most one point), every
944	  nonempty open subset equals the whole space.
945	\end{lemma}
946	
947	\begin{proof}
948	  Proved directly in Lean: the unique-point argument. A sub-step of
949	  \cref{lem:gammaSpecField_ringEquiv}.
950	\end{proof}
951	
952	\begin{lemma}\leanok
953	[Step B.c --- sections of $\Spec \bar k$ over a nonempty open are $\bar k$]
954	  \label{lem:gammaSpecField_ringEquiv}
955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.gammaSpecField_ringEquiv}
956	  \uses{lem:open_eq_top_of_subsingleton}
957	  For a field \(\bar k\) and any nonempty open \(U\) of \(\Spec(\bar k)\), the section
958	  ring \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k, U)\) is ring-isomorphic to \(\bar k\). This gives the
959	  algebraically-closed base ring of the standard-smooth presentation a clean
960	  \(\bar k\)-identification.
961	\end{lemma}
962	
963	\begin{proof}
964	  Proved directly in Lean: \(\Spec(\bar k)\) has a single point, so
965	  \cref{lem:open_eq_top_of_subsingleton} forces \(U = \top\) and the sections collapse
966	  to the global sections \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k, \top) \simeq \bar k\).
967	\end{proof}
968	
969	\paragraph{Matsumura regular-sequence bridge (Stacks 00NQ).}
970	
971	\begin{lemma}\leanok
972	[Step A1 bridge --- module quotient equals ring quotient]
973	  \label{lem:quotSMulTop_quotientRing_linearEquiv}
974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.quotSMulTop_quotientRing_linearEquiv}
975	  For \(r\) in a commutative ring \(A\), there is a canonical \((A/(r))\)-linear
976	  isomorphism between the module quotient \(A/(r \cdot A)\) and the ring quotient
977	  \(A/(r)\).
978	\end{lemma}
979	
980	\begin{proof}
981	  Proved directly in Lean: compose the tensor description of the module quotient with
982	  the right-unitor and promote scalars along the surjective quotient map. A sub-step of
983	  \cref{lem:isRegular_cons_of_quotient_ring}.
984	\end{proof}
985	
986	\begin{lemma}\leanok
987	[Step A1 bridge --- ring-quotient cons rule for regular sequences]
988	  \label{lem:isRegular_cons_of_quotient_ring}
989	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegular_cons_of_quotient_ring}
990	  \uses{lem:quotSMulTop_quotientRing_linearEquiv}
991	  Let \(r \in A\) be such that multiplication by \(r\) is injective on \(A\), and
992	  suppose the images of a list \(rs\) form a regular sequence over the ring quotient
993	  \(A/(r)\). Then \(r :: rs\) is a regular sequence over \(A\).
994	\end{lemma}
995	
996	\begin{proof}
997	  Proved directly in Lean: the module-quotient cons rule transported across the
998	  identification \cref{lem:quotSMulTop_quotientRing_linearEquiv}. A sub-step of
999	  \cref{lem:matsumura_isRegular_of_linearIndependent_cotangent}.
1000	\end{proof}
1001	
1002	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1003	[Step A1 --- cotangent independence descends to the quotient]
1004	  \label{lem:matsumura_descent_cotangent}
1005	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.matsumura_descent_cotangent}
1006	  Let \((A, \mathfrak m)\) be a local ring and \(f_0,\dots,f_m \in \mathfrak m\) with
1007	  \(\kappa(A)\)-linearly independent cotangent classes. Then the tail
1008	  \(f_1,\dots,f_m\), pushed into the quotient \(A' = A/(f_0)\), has
1009	  \(\kappa(A')\)-linearly independent cotangent classes in
1010	  \(\mathfrak m'/\mathfrak m'^2\).
1011	\end{lemma}
1012	
1013	\begin{proof}
1014	  Proved directly in Lean: the cotangent map \(A \to A'\) is surjective with kernel
1015	  spanned by the class of \(f_0\); a kernel-disjointness argument reduces a
1016	  \(\kappa(A')\)-relation to a \(\kappa(A)\)-relation killed by the full independence
1017	  hypothesis. A sub-step of
1018	  \cref{lem:matsumura_isRegular_of_linearIndependent_cotangent}.
1019	\end{proof}
1020	
1021	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1022	[Step A1 --- Matsumura: cotangent-independent elements form a regular sequence]
1023	  \label{lem:matsumura_isRegular_of_linearIndependent_cotangent}
1024	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.matsumura_isRegular_of_linearIndependent_cotangent}
1025	  \uses{lem:matsumura_descent_cotangent, lem:isRegular_cons_of_quotient_ring}
1026	  Let \((A, \mathfrak m)\) be a Noetherian regular local ring and \(f_1,\dots,f_n \in
1027	  \mathfrak m\) whose cotangent classes in \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\) are
1028	  \(\kappa(A)\)-linearly independent. Then \(f_1,\dots,f_n\) form a regular sequence in
1029	  \(A\) (Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Thm.~14.2; Stacks 00NQ).
1030	\end{lemma}
1031	
1032	\begin{proof}
1033	  Proved directly in Lean: induction on \(n\). The head \(f_1\) is a non-zero-divisor
1034	  (a regular local ring is a domain), the cotangent independence descends to the
1035	  quotient \(A/(f_1)\) by \cref{lem:matsumura_descent_cotangent}, and the
1036	  ring-quotient cons rule \cref{lem:isRegular_cons_of_quotient_ring} reassembles the
1037	  regular sequence.
1038	\end{proof}
1039	
1040	\subsection{Regularity at arbitrary primes (Serre-free Stacks 00TT)}
1041	\label{subsec:smooth_prime_regularity}
1042	
1043	The closed-point regularity of \cref{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_closed_point_algclosed}
1044	extends to \emph{every} prime of a standard-smooth algebra over a perfect
1045	field, without invoking the localisation theorem for regular local rings
1046	(Stacks tag 00OF, Serre's homological characterisation). The route combines a
1047	transcendence-degree/height inequality in polynomial rings with the conormal
1048	sequence at an arbitrary prime.
1049	
1050	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1051	[One-variable step of the trdeg--height inequality]
1052	  \label{lem:polynomial_step_height_trdeg}
1053	  \lean{Polynomial.step_height_trdeg_of_isPrime}
1054	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00ON (dimension formula for going-down
1055	  % extensions, Matsumura 13.B Theorem 19(2)), used through Mathlib's
1056	  % Ideal.height_eq_height_add_of_liesOver_of_hasGoingDown.
1057	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(R\) a Noetherian \(k\)-algebra, \(P \subseteq R[X]\)
1058	  a prime ideal, and \(p = P \cap R\). Then either
1059	  \[\operatorname{ht} p + 1 \le \operatorname{ht} P
1060	    \quad\text{and}\quad
1061	    \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R/p) \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R[X]/P),\]
1062	  or
1063	  \[\operatorname{ht} P = \operatorname{ht} p
1064	    \quad\text{and}\quad
1065	    \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R/p) + 1 \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R[X]/P).\]
1066	\end{lemma}
1067	
1068	\begin{proof}
1069	  \leanok
1070	  The extension \(R \to R[X]\) is free, hence flat, hence satisfies going-down,
1071	  so the dimension formula (Stacks 00ON) gives
1072	  \(\operatorname{ht} P = \operatorname{ht} p + \operatorname{ht} \bar P\) for
1073	  the image \(\bar P\) of \(P\) in \(R[X]/pR[X] \cong (R/p)[X]\). Write
1074	  \(A = R/p\) (a domain) and \(Q \subseteq A[X]\) for the prime corresponding
1075	  to \(\bar P\); then \(Q \cap A = 0\) and \(A[X]/Q \cong R[X]/P\) as
1076	  \(k\)-algebras. If \(Q = 0\), the heights agree and
1077	  \(R[X]/P \cong A[X]\); superadditivity of transcendence degree in the tower
1078	  \(k \to A \to A[X]\) together with \(\operatorname{trdeg}_A A[X] = 1\) gives
1079	  \(\operatorname{trdeg}_k A[X] \ge \operatorname{trdeg}_k A + 1\). If
1080	  \(Q \neq 0\), then \(0 \subsetneq Q\) is a chain of primes in the domain
1081	  \(A[X]\), so \(\operatorname{ht} \bar P = \operatorname{ht} Q \ge 1\), while
1082	  \(A\) still embeds into \(A[X]/Q\) because \(Q \cap A = 0\), whence
1083	  \(\operatorname{trdeg}_k A \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (A[X]/Q)\).
1084	\end{proof}
1085	
1086	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1087	[trdeg--height inequality at primes of polynomial rings]
1088	  \label{lem:mvpoly_trdeg_height}
1089	  \lean{MvPolynomial.exists_le_trdeg_and_natCard_le_height_add}
1090	  \uses{lem:polynomial_step_height_trdeg}
1091	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(\iota\) a finite set, and
1092	  \(P \subseteq k[x_i : i \in \iota]\) a prime ideal. Then there is
1093	  \(d \in \mathbb N\) with
1094	  \[d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k \bigl(k[x_i]/P\bigr)
1095	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
1096	    \#\iota \le \operatorname{ht} P + d.\]
1097	  (Classically \(d\) may be taken to be the transcendence degree of the residue
1098	  field and both inequalities are equalities; the witness form suffices for the
1099	  regularity pipeline and requires no Noether normalisation.)
1100	\end{lemma}
1101	
1102	\begin{proof}
1103	  \leanok
1104	  Induction on \(\#\iota\). If \(\iota = \emptyset\) take \(d = 0\). For the
1105	  step, write \(k[x_i : i \in \iota] = R[X]\) with
1106	  \(R = k[x_i : i \in \iota\setminus\{i_0\}]\) and contract \(P\) to
1107	  \(p = P \cap R\); the inductive hypothesis provides \(d\) with
1108	  \(d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R/p)\) and \(\#\iota - 1 \le
1109	  \operatorname{ht} p + d\). In the height-jump case of
1110	  \cref{lem:polynomial_step_height_trdeg},
1111	  \(\#\iota \le (\operatorname{ht} p + 1) + d \le \operatorname{ht} P + d\)
1112	  and the same \(d\) works; in the height-preserving case,
1113	  \(\#\iota \le \operatorname{ht} P + (d+1)\) and
1114	  \(d + 1 \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R[X]/P)\), so \(d+1\) works.
1115	\end{proof}
1116	
1117	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1118	[trdeg--height inequality for standard-smooth algebras]
1119	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_trdeg_height}
1120	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.exists_le_trdeg_and_natCast_le_height_add}
1121	  \uses{lem:mvpoly_trdeg_height}
1122	  Let \(S\) be a standard-smooth algebra of relative dimension \(n\) over a
1123	  field \(k\) and \(q \subseteq S\) a prime ideal. Then there is
1124	  \(d \in \mathbb N\) with \(d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (S/q)\) and
1125	  \(n \le \operatorname{ht} q + d\).
1126	\end{lemma}
1127	
1128	\begin{proof}
1129	  \leanok
1130	  Choose a submersive presentation \(k[x_i : i \in \iota] \twoheadrightarrow S\)
1131	  whose kernel is generated by \(\#\sigma\) relations, with
1132	  \(n = \#\iota - \#\sigma\). Let \(M\) be the preimage of \(q\); then
1133	  \(k[x_i]/M \cong S/q\), and \cref{lem:mvpoly_trdeg_height} provides \(d\)
1134	  with \(d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (S/q)\) and
1135	  \(\#\iota \le \operatorname{ht} M + d\). The relative form of Krull's height
1136	  theorem bounds \(\operatorname{ht} M \le \operatorname{ht} q + \#\sigma\)
1137	  (the same argument as in \cref{lem:standard_smooth_le_height_of_isMaximal});
1138	  cancelling \(\#\sigma\) gives \(n \le \operatorname{ht} q + d\).
1139	\end{proof}
1140	
1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[K\"ahler rank equals transcendence degree over a perfect field]
1143	  \label{lem:kaehler_rank_eq_trdeg}
1144	  \lean{Algebra.rank_kaehlerDifferential_eq_trdeg}
1145	  Let \(k\) be a perfect field and \(K\) a field extension of \(k\) that is
1146	  essentially of finite type. Then
1147	  \[\dim_K \Omega_{K/k} = \operatorname{trdeg}_k K.\]
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \leanok
1152	  Since \(k\) is perfect, \(K\) admits a separating transcendence basis \(s\):
1153	  writing \(F = k(s)\) for the rational-function subfield, the extension
1154	  \(K/F\) is separable algebraic, hence formally \'etale, so base change along
1155	  \(F \to K\) identifies \(\Omega_{K/k} \cong K \otimes_F \Omega_{F/k}\).
1156	  Moreover \(F\) is the fraction field of the polynomial ring \(k[s]\), so
1157	  \(\Omega_{F/k}\) is the localisation of \(\Omega_{k[s]/k}\), which is free
1158	  with basis \(\{dx_i : i \in s\}\). Hence
1159	  \(\dim_K \Omega_{K/k} = \#s = \operatorname{trdeg}_k K\).
1160	\end{proof}
1161	
1162	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1163	[Conormal dimension identity at an arbitrary prime]
1164	  \label{lem:conormal_finrank_identity}
1165	  \lean{finrank_cotangentSpace_add_finrank_kaehler_residueField}
1166	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02JK (the conormal sequence of a formally
1167	  % smooth local algebra).
1168	  Let \((S_{\mathfrak m}, \mathfrak m, \kappa)\) be a local \(R\)-algebra that
1169	  is formally smooth over \(R\), whose residue field \(\kappa\) is also
1170	  formally smooth over \(R\), and whose K\"ahler module
1171	  \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak m}/R}\) is free of rank \(n\). Then
1172	  \[\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \dim_\kappa \Omega_{\kappa/R} = n.\]
1173	\end{lemma}
1174	
1175	\begin{proof}
1176	  \leanok
1177	  The conormal sequence
1178	  \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \to \kappa \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak m}}
1179	  \Omega_{S_{\mathfrak m}/R} \to \Omega_{\kappa/R} \to 0\)
1180	  is exact, and formal smoothness of \(\kappa\) over \(R\) provides a
1181	  retraction of its first map, which is therefore (split) injective. The
1182	  middle term has \(\kappa\)-dimension \(n\) by freeness, and the last map is
1183	  surjective; rank--nullity gives the identity.
1184	\end{proof}

1196	  % here without tag 00OF (localisation of regular local rings).
1197	  Let \(k\) be a perfect field, \(S\) a standard-smooth \(k\)-algebra of
1198	  relative dimension \(n\), and \(q \subseteq S\) any prime ideal. Then any
1199	  localisation \(S_q\) of \(S\) at \(q\) is a regular local ring.
1200	\end{theorem}

1202	\begin{proof}
1203	  \leanok
1204	  Write \(\kappa\) for the residue field of \(S_q\). The K\"ahler module
1205	  \(\Omega_{S_q/k}\) is free of rank \(n\) (the localisation of the free
1206	  module \(\Omega_{S/k}\), \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization} and
1207	  \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}); \(S_q\) is formally
1208	  smooth over \(k\) (localisations of standard-smooth algebras are formally
1209	  smooth), and \(\kappa\) is formally smooth over \(k\) since it is an
1210	  essentially-finite-type field extension of a perfect field (separating
1211	  transcendence bases exist). By \cref{lem:conormal_finrank_identity},
1212	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 = n - \dim_\kappa \Omega_{\kappa/k}\).
1213	  By \cref{lem:kaehler_rank_eq_trdeg} and the embedding of \(S/q\) into
1214	  \(\kappa\), \(\dim_\kappa \Omega_{\kappa/k} = \operatorname{trdeg}_k \kappa
1215	  \ge \operatorname{trdeg}_k (S/q) \ge d\) for the witness \(d\) of
1216	  \cref{lem:standard_smooth_trdeg_height}, which also gives
1217	  \(n \le \operatorname{ht} q + d\). Hence
1218	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \le n - d \le \operatorname{ht} q
1219	  = \dim S_q\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime}), and a
1220	  Noetherian local ring whose cotangent dimension is at most its Krull
1221	  dimension is regular (\cref{lem:regular_of_finrank_cotangent_le_dim}).
1222	\end{proof}

1333	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
1334	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
1335	
1336	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
1337	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
1338	point.
1339	
1340	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1341	[Milne Theorem~3.1, codim-\(\geq 2\) conclusion]
1342	  \label{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}
1343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_codimGe2_of_smooth_of_complete}
1344	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
1345	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
1346	  % valuative-criterion half of the proof).
1347	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), \(Y\) a complete variety
1348	  over \(\bar k\), and \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) a rational map of
1349	  \(\bar k\)-varieties (i.e.\ defined over \(\bar k\): \(f\) followed by the
1350	  structure morphism of \(Y\) is the structure morphism of \(X\), as rational
1351	  maps). Then every point of the indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\)
1352	  (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has coheight at least \(2\); that is,
1353	  \(Z(f)\) contains no point of codimension \(\le 1\).
1354	\end{lemma}
1355	
1356	\begin{proof}
1357	  \leanok
1358	  The generic point of \(X\) lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\), which is a dense
1359	  open, so no point of coheight \(0\) lies in \(Z(f)\). Suppose \(z \in Z(f)\)
1360	  had coheight \(1\). By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} the stalk
1361	  \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\) is a discrete valuation ring with fraction field the
1362	  function field \(K(X)\). The rational map \(f\) determines a morphism
1363	  \(\Spec K(X) \to Y\), which together with the canonical morphism
1364	  \(\Spec \mathcal O_{X,z} \to \Spec \bar k\) forms a commutative valuative
1365	  square over the structure morphism of \(Y\) --- the square commutes
1366	  because \(f\) is defined over \(\bar k\). The valuative criterion of
1367	  properness for the complete variety \(Y\) produces a lift
1368	  \(\Spec \mathcal O_{X,z} \to Y\). Spreading out (using that \(X\) is
1369	  integral, hence germ-injective, and \(Y\) is of finite type over
1370	  \(\bar k\)), \(f\) admits a representative defined on an open set
1371	  containing \(z\), contradicting \(z \in Z(f)\).
1372	\end{proof}
1373	
1374	
1375	\begin{corollary}\leanok
1376	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-freeness]
1377	  \label{cor:codim_one_free}
1378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.codimOneFree_of_smooth_of_complete}
1379	  \uses{def:codim_one_indeterminacy, thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}
1380	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.
1381	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety
1382	  over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map of
1383	  \(\bar k\)-varieties. Then \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1384	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}): every codimension-\(1\) point of \(X\) lies in
1385	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
1386	\end{corollary}
1387	
1388	\begin{proof}
1389	  \leanok
1390	  Immediate from \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}: a codimension-\(1\) point of the
1391	  indeterminacy locus would have coheight \(1 < 2\).
1392	\end{proof}

1394	\begin{theorem}\leanok
1395	  [Extension of a rational map with empty indeterminacy locus]
1396	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
1397	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.existsUnique_hom_of_indeterminacyLocus_eq_empty}
1398	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus}
1399	  Let \(X\) be a reduced scheme, let \(Y\) be a separated scheme, and let
1400	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map whose indeterminacy locus
1401	  (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) is empty, i.e.\ \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\). Then there
1402	  is a \emph{unique} morphism \(g \colon X \to Y\) whose associated rational map is
1403	  \(f\).
1404	\end{theorem}
1405	
1406	\begin{proof}
1407	  \leanok
1408	  \emph{Existence.} A rational map is an equivalence class of partial maps defined on
1409	  dense opens, two partial maps being equivalent when they agree on a common dense
1410	  open. Because \(X\) is reduced and \(Y\) is separated, any two representatives agree
1411	  on the \emph{whole} intersection of their domains (the locus where two morphisms to
1412	  a separated scheme agree is closed, and it contains a dense open of the reduced
1413	  intersection), so all representatives glue to a partial map defined on the union
1414	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\) of all their domains. By hypothesis \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\), so
1415	  this maximal representative is a morphism \(g \colon X \to Y\), and its rational
1416	  class is \(f\) by construction.
1417	
1418	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Two morphisms \(X \to Y\) with the same associated rational map
1419	  agree on a common dense open. Since \(X\) is reduced and \(Y\) is separated, they
1420	  agree everywhere.
1421	\end{proof}
1422	
1423	\begin{remark}
1424	  \label{rmk:codim1_no_extension_from_codim1free}
1425	  \textbf{Codim-\(1\)-freeness alone does not give an extension.} For a merely
1426	  \emph{complete} target, a rational map that is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free need
1427	  \emph{not} extend to a morphism: the projection
1428	  \(\mathbb P^2 \dashrightarrow \mathbb P^1\), \([x:y:z] \mapsto [x:y]\), away from the
1429	  point \(p = [0:0:1]\) has indeterminacy locus exactly \(\{p\}\), a single closed
1430	  point of codimension \(2\) --- so it is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free --- yet admits
1431	  no regular extension to \(\mathbb P^2\) (one must blow up \(p\)). Consequently the
1432	  extension step of Milne's Theorem~3.2 cannot run through
1433	  codim-\(1\)-freeness for a general complete target; it needs the indeterminacy locus
1434	  to be genuinely \emph{empty}, which is what combining
1435	  \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2} with the pure-codim-\(1\) dichotomy of
1436	  \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (available because an abelian variety is a
1437	  \emph{group} variety) delivers. \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} then finishes.
1438	
1439	  For the same reason, no depth or local-cohomology input
1440	  (Auslander--Buchsbaum, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}) enters this chain:
1441	  \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2} is purely valuative, and
1442	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is formal once \(Z(f) = \emptyset\). A depth-\(\geq 2\)
1443	  local-cohomology argument would be needed only for Weil-style extension theorems
1444	  across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (rational maps into \emph{group} varieties defined in
1445	  codimension \(1\)), which Milne's route deliberately avoids.
1446	\end{remark}
1447	
1448	\end{remark}

1451	\label{sec:milne_lem33}

1453	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
1454	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
1455	Combined with \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
1456	this forces the locus to be empty, and \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} then extends.

1528	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
1529	    \qquad
1530	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
1531	  \]
1532	  via the composition
1533	  \[
1534	    U \times U
1535	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
1536	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
1537	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
1538	  \]
1539	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
1540	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
1541	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
1542	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
1543	
1544	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
1545	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
1546	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
1547	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
1548	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
1549	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
1550	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
1551	  identity
1552	  \[
1553	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
1554	    \qquad u \in W',
1555	  \]
1556	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence \(x \in
1557	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\) if and only if \((x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\).
1558	
1559	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
1560	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
1561	  \(K(X \times X)\):
1562	  \[
1563	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
1564	  \]
1565	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
1566	  \((x, x)\) if and only if \(\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
1567	  \times X, (x, x)}\) (i.e.\ every local function on \(G\) near \(e\) has no pole at
1568	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
1569	
1570	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
1571	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
1572	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
1573	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
1574	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
1575	  \[
1576	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
1577	  \]
1578	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
1579	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
1580	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
1581	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
1582	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
1583	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
1584	  \[
1585	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
1586	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
1587	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1588	  \]
1589	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1590	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1591	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1592	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1593	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1594	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1595	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1596	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1597	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1598	
1599	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1600	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some \(f_0 \in
1601	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\) with \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)\).
1602	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection \(\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1603	  \Delta_X\) is pure codimension \(1\) in \(\Delta_X\), passing through \((x, x)\).
1604	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1605	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1606	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1607	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1608	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1609	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1610	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1611	\end{proof}
1612	
1613	\begin{remark}
1614	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1615	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1616	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1617	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1618	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1619	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1620	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1621	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1622	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1623	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1624	\end{remark}
1625	
1626	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1627	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1628	
1629	The proof of \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2} (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1630	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1631	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1632	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1633	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1634	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1635	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1636	
1637	
1638	\begin{remark}
1639	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
1640	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
1641	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
1642	  \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
1643	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
1644	  \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2} suffices and \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1645	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
1646	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
1647	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
1648	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
1649	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
1650	\end{remark}
1651	
1652	
1653	\begin{remark}
1654	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
1655	  \textbf{Relationship to \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
1656	  \texttt{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} records the definitional half of
1657	  the Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole'' reformulation that
1658	  \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
1659	  form: it provides a partial-map representative through a prime-divisor generic
1660	  point. The full \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form additionally requires the pullback of a
1661	  rational map to the function field, which is the content of
1662	  \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.
1663	\end{remark}
1664	
1665	\section{Lean encoding}
1666	\label{sec:codim1_lean_encoding}
1667	
1668	The chapter's declarations correspond, in order, to the following Lean targets:
1669	
1670	\begin{enumerate}
1671	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} ---
1672	    \cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}, a method on the Mathlib
1673	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure. Implementation: \texttt{X.carrier
1674	    \textbackslash{} f.domain} as a \texttt{Set X}, paired with an
1675	    \texttt{IsClosed} witness (either bundled or as a separate
1676	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus} lemma).
1677	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} ---
1678	    \cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}, a \texttt{Prop}-valued predicate. Encoding:
1679	    \texttt{\(\forall\) \(\eta\) : X, Order.coheight \(\eta\) = 1 \(\to\) \(\eta\) \(\in\)
1680	    f.domain} (matching the \texttt{Order.coheight} idiom of
1681	    \cref{def:prime_divisor}).
1682	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} ---
1683	    \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, a lemma (or instance) producing
1684	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing (Scheme.LocalRing.at\ X \(\eta\))} under the
1685	    typeclass set \texttt{[Smooth (\(\Spec \bar k\)) X] [IsIntegral X]} and the
1686	    hypothesis \texttt{Order.coheight \(\eta\) = 1}.
1687	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.existsUnique\_hom\_of\_indeterminacyLocus\_eq\_empty}
1688	    --- \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, the existence-of-extension theorem. Signature
1689	    (informal): given a reduced scheme \(X\), a separated scheme \(Y\), and
1690	    \(f\) with empty indeterminacy locus, produce a unique regular morphism
1691	    \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\) representing \(f\). (Codim-\(1\)-freeness alone
1692	    would \emph{not} suffice; see \cref{rmk:codim1_no_extension_from_codim1free}.)
1693	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy\_pure\_codim\_one\_into\_grpScheme}
1694	    --- \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}. Signature (informal): given a smooth
1695	    variety \(X\), a group scheme \(G\) over \(\bar k\) that is a variety (smooth, finite
1696	    type, separated), and \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) rational, conclude that
1697	    \(Z(f)\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
1698	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain\_iff\_exists\_partialMap\_through\_point}
1699	    --- the definitional reshuffle pinned at
1700	    \texttt{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}. The substantive
1701	    Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole'' Weil-divisor reformulation lives
1702	    at \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}. Re-uses
1703	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order} from
1704	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} (the order map at a prime divisor).
1705	\end{enumerate}
1706	
1707	
1708	\section{Out of scope}
1709	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1710	
1711	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1712	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1713	scope:
1714	\begin{itemize}
1715	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1716	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1717	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1718	    chapter no longer consumes them (see
1719	    \cref{rmk:codim1_no_extension_from_codim1free}: no depth input is needed once
1720	    the indeterminacy locus is empty).
1721	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1722	    \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}, \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} and
1723	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} into the
1724	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1725	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1726	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1727	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1728	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1729	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1730	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1731	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1732	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1733	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1734	    (\texttt{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1735	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1736	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1737	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1738	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1739	    \texttt{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1740	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1741	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1742	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1743	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1744	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1745	    introduced.
1746	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1747	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1748	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1749	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1750	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1751	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1752	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2} and
1753	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1754	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1755	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1756	\end{itemize}
1757

data.content:
======= REPORT =======
Everything is clean. The working-tree state matches session start (untracked-at-root as before), scratch removed, and the scope files are at their target state.

## Summary

- **Found and removed a false pinned statement**: `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` claimed "CodimOneFree ⇒ unique regular extension" for any complete target — false (counterexample `ℙ² ⇢ ℙ¹` from a point: CodimOneFree, no extension). Its `sorry` was mathematically unclosable and had silently blocked the Thm 3.2 chain for ~30 iterations.
- **Proved the true extension theorem** `existsUnique_hom_of_indeterminacyLocus_eq_empty` (empty indeterminacy locus ⇒ unique regular representative; reduced source, separated target), axiom-clean, plus helpers `hom_ext_of_toRationalMap_eq`, `toPartialMap_domain`, and Milne 3.1's `CodimOneFree` corollary `codimOneFree_of_smooth_of_complete`.
- **Rewired Milne 3.2** (`extend_to_av`): now sorry-free in body, `sorryAx` only via Milne 3.3. Albanese scope sorries: `Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean` 0, `CodimOneExtension.lean` 2 → **1** (only Milne 3.3 remains).

## Progress

- Strengthened Milne 3.3's Lean statement: codim-1 witness must lie in `Z(f)` (old form was nearly vacuous — could not force emptiness).
- Deleted the three dead `CodimOneFree` helpers in Thm32; added `av_indeterminacyLocus_eq_empty` (3.3 + codim-≥2 ⇒ `Z(f) = ∅`).
- Blueprint reconciled: `thm:codim_one_extension` restated/repinned (fully `\leanok`), new `cor:codim_one_free` and counterexample remark, Thm32 nodes/`\uses` rewired, phantom 0AVF/Auslander–Buchsbaum "Step 2" dependency purged, `lean_decls` updated.
- Checks run: full `lake build` green (8582 jobs); `#print axioms` clean on all new lemmas; `horizon blueprint` refresh — Albanese 299 nodes, 289 proved, 0 dangling.
- Inbox: closed `I-0059` (subsumed) and `I-0058` (verified already fixed); commented `I-0049`; filed `I-0064` (info) and `I-0065` (memory); roadmap comment on `ALB.codim1`.

## Issues

- **Milne 3.3** (`indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`) is the single remaining sorry of this chain — needs difference-map + function-field-pullback + pole-divisor machinery; not attempted (multi-session build).
- Pre-existing `checkdecls` debt: 10 `lean_decls` entries fail (Genus0/normalization `phi_left_*`, `genusZero_curve_iso_P1`, `chartAway_*`, private `ChartIso.lean` pins) — untouched by this session, noted in `I-0058`'s closing comment.
- The 0AVF/depth-≥2 "blocker" previously reported for `ALB.codim1` is retired — it only ever served the false statement.

## Next

- Attack Milne 3.3 as its own task (substeps already sketched in-file; substantive gap is the pole-divisor/diagonal codim-1 lemma).
- Ground: sweep older status prose citing `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth`/0AVF elsewhere in the workspace (e.g. `AlbaneseUP.lean` header, HTML blueprint is stale until re-render).
- Optional cleanup task: fix the 10 pre-existing Genus0 `lean_decls`/private-pin failures.

======= TASK (look for task file) =======

1027	+  by_contra hnotin

1032	+/-- The maximal representative `RationalMap.toPartialMap` of a rational map

1037	+    (f : X.RationalMap Y) : f.toPartialMap.domain = f.domain := rfl
1038	+
1039	+set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
1040	+/-- Morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme are determined by
1041	+their rational-map class: if `g₁.toRationalMap = g₂.toRationalMap` then
1042	+`g₁ = g₂`. This is the reduced-and-separated agreement principle

1044	+equivalence: the two morphisms agree on a common dense open. -/
1045	+theorem hom_ext_of_toRationalMap_eq {X Y : Scheme.{u}} [IsReduced X]
1046	+    [Y.IsSeparated] {g₁ g₂ : X ⟶ Y}
1047	+    (e : g₁.toRationalMap = g₂.toRationalMap) : g₁ = g₂ := by
1048	+  obtain ⟨W, hW, hle₁, hle₂, he⟩ := PartialMap.toRationalMap_eq_iff.mp e
1049	+  haveI : IsDominant W.ι := Opens.isDominant_ι hW
1050	+  exact ext_of_isDominant W.ι (by simpa [Scheme.Hom.toPartialMap] using he)
1051	+
1052	+set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
1053	+/-- **Extension of a rational map with empty indeterminacy locus.** If the
1054	+indeterminacy locus of a rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` from a reduced scheme to a
1055	+separated scheme is empty, then `f` is (uniquely) represented by a regular
1056	+morphism `g : X ⟶ Y`.
1057	+
1058	+This is the honest "extension" content of the Milne §I.3 chain: once
1059	+`Z(f) = ∅` — which for an abelian-variety target follows from combining
1060	+Milne Lemma 3.3 (`indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`) with the
1061	+codim-≥2 conclusion of Milne Theorem 3.1

1063	+`f.toPartialMap` is defined on all of `X`, and its composite with the
1064	+isomorphism `X ≅ (⊤ : X.Opens)` is the required morphism. Uniqueness is the
1065	+reduced-and-separated agreement principle (`hom_ext_of_toRationalMap_eq`).
1066	+
1067	+**Statement-correction note (run-0006 T6, session 0015).** This lemma
1068	+*replaces* the former `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth`, whose statement —
1069	+"`CodimOneFree f` alone implies a regular extension, for an arbitrary
1070	+complete target" — was **false as stated**: the projection `ℙ² ⇢ ℙ¹` away
1071	+from a point satisfies every hypothesis (smooth integral source, complete
1072	+smooth integral target, indeterminacy a single codim-2 closed point, hence
1073	+`CodimOneFree`), yet admits no regular extension (any extension `g` would
1074	+force `⊤ = g.toPartialMap.domain ≤ f.domain` via
1075	+`PartialMap.le_domain_toRationalMap`, but `f.domain = ℙ² ∖ {pt}`). Milne 3.1
1076	+asserts only the codim-≥2 conclusion; the everywhere-extension in Milne 3.2

1080	+theorem existsUnique_hom_of_indeterminacyLocus_eq_empty
1081	+    {X Y : Scheme.{u}} [IsReduced X] [Y.IsSeparated]
1082	+    (f : X.RationalMap Y) (h : indeterminacyLocus f = ∅) :
1083	+    ∃! (g : X ⟶ Y), g.toRationalMap = f := by
1084	+  -- The domain of definition is all of `X`.
1085	+  have hdom : f.toPartialMap.domain = ⊤ := by
1086	+    rw [toPartialMap_domain, ← TopologicalSpace.Opens.coe_inj,
1087	+      TopologicalSpace.Opens.coe_top, ← Set.compl_empty_iff]
1088	+    exact h
1089	+  -- The candidate morphism: transport the maximal representative along
1090	+  -- `X ≅ ⊤ ≅ f.toPartialMap.domain`.
1091	+  have hwit : (X.topIso.inv ≫ X.homOfLE hdom.ge
1092	+      ≫ f.toPartialMap.hom).toRationalMap = f := by
1093	+    conv_rhs => rw [← f.toRationalMap_toPartialMap]
1094	+    refine PartialMap.toRationalMap_eq_iff.mpr ?_
1095	+    refine ⟨⊤, by rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.coe_top]; exact dense_univ,
1096	+      le_top, hdom.ge, ?_⟩
1097	+    simp only [PartialMap.restrict_hom, Scheme.Hom.toPartialMap,
1098	+      Scheme.topIso_hom, Scheme.ι_toIso_inv_assoc,
1099	+      Scheme.homOfLE_homOfLE_assoc]
1100	+  exact ⟨_, hwit, fun g hg => hom_ext_of_toRationalMap_eq (hg.trans hwit.symm)⟩

text:
Base directory for this skill: /AI4M/users/Axel/LeanAlgebraicGeometry-Horizon/.claude/skills/leandag

The DAG links blueprint nodes by `\uses` edges. An edge `source -> target` means
*target depends on source*. The engine records each node's `\lean` link,
`proved`/`leanok` status, effort metrics, and Lean source metadata.

## Keep It Fresh

- Run the project build after Lean/blueprint edits so generated Lean artifacts and
  imported libraries are current.
- Run `horizon blueprint` after blueprint or Lean declaration changes; it writes
  `.archon-horizon/blueprints/<project>.json` for the dashboard and cached DAG
  consumers.
- Use `horizon leandag` for live inspection when deciding what to prove next.

## `horizon leandag` (preferred)

- `horizon leandag` — per-project summary: nodes, edges, proved count, and any
  isolated nodes (no edge in or out — usually a blueprint bug to fix).
- `horizon leandag -p <project> --node <label>` — one node's direct deps, full
  transitive cone, and reverse deps.
- `horizon leandag -p a -p b --union` — merge projects by declaration/node id.
  Same-id nodes are combined; divergent non-signature metadata is tolerated, but
  a Lean signature mismatch is an error to investigate.
- `horizon leandag -p a -p b --intersect` — only shared declaration/node ids,
  with the same signature check.
- Add `--json` for machine-readable output.

A node is "ready" when all dependencies are proved. Prefer ready nodes when
choosing what to attempt next.

## Cached JSON

`horizon blueprint` writes each project's DAG to
`.archon-horizon/blueprints/<project>.json` (`nodes`, `edges`, `dangling`) for
the dashboard. Under the leandag engine, `\uses` are resolved at build/cache
time, so `dangling` is empty there; the parser fallback still populates it. To
check for orphan/unresolved nodes, use `horizon leandag` rather than relying only
on `dangling`.
